assignment.name: dummy
assignment.title: Build a small web app that visualizes city air-quality data
assignment.description: Build a single-page web application that fetches public air-quality measurements for a chosen city and presents them visually. The app should let a user pick a city, see current AQI, and view a 7-day trend.
constraints: Must be a single-page web app (no multi-page navigation).
Must use a free public air-quality data source.
Must run locally with a single command (no paid hosting required).
Must include at least one chart/visualization.

verdict.notes: Solid, well-scoped plan with proven tools and realistic scope. Main risk: Open-AQ's API response times and data availability vary by region—test city selection early. Consider pre-selecting 2-3 reliable cities if API proves inconsistent.

branches.option: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)

verdict.notes: Plan is solid and well-scoped: OpenWeatherMap free tier provides reliable historical data, React+Vite is efficient for SPAs, and Chart.js is proven for this use case. Main risks are API rate limits (requires careful caching) and ensuring 7-day historical data is accessible on the free tier—verify API docs early to confirm historical endpoints are available without paid upgrade.

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)

verdict.notes: Plan is solid and well-scoped for the constraints. OpenWeatherMap's free tier supports current AQI + historical data needed for 7-day trends, Chart.js is proven lightweight choice, and vanilla JS keeps complexity low. Main risk: OpenWeatherMap's historical data availability on free tier may require fallback to synthetic trend data or switching to WAQI API—verify data source depth before committing 2+ days to implementation.

branches.option: D3.js (powerful but steeper learning curve, larger)

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)
D3.js (powerful but steeper learning curve, larger)

verdict.notes: The plan is feasible but has a critical risk: D3.js introduces significant overhead for a simple 7-day trend chart, likely consuming 200+ lines just for basic setup and pushing toward the 1500-line limit. Consider swapping D3.js for Chart.js or a lightweight alternative (Plotly.js, ApexCharts) to preserve code budget and reduce learning friction, while still meeting all requirements comfortably.

branches.option: Inline SVG/Canvas (no dependency, more code to write)

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)
Inline SVG/Canvas (no dependency, more code to write)

verdict.notes: The plan is feasible and meets all constraints, but rolling custom SVG/Canvas visualization will consume ~20% of effort and risk visual polish issues under time pressure. Consider Chart.js (lightweight, single file) as a pragmatic middle ground that preserves the no-build-step advantage while freeing time for data handling and UX refinement.

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)

verdict.notes: Solid tech choices with proven libraries, but OpenWeatherMap's free tier has rate limits and may require careful API key management. Plan should explicitly address: (1) fallback/mock data strategy if API calls fail, (2) local storage for caching 7-day history since the API doesn't inherently provide historical data—this is the highest risk, requiring daily snapshots or a workaround, and (3) whether Recharts is truly 'React-native' (it's a React charting library, which is fine, just verify no native dependencies complicate local setup).

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)

verdict.notes: The tech stack is solid and well-matched to constraints, but Plotly.js adds unnecessary weight for a simple 7-day trend chart—consider Chart.js or Recharts instead to keep bundle size down and stay comfortably under 1500 lines. API integration and React/Vite setup are straightforward; main risk is handling API rate limits and missing cities gracefully.

history: OpenWeatherMap Air Pollution API (free tier with API key)

verdict.notes: Solid technical choices with realistic scope, but OpenWeatherMap free tier has limited historical data (current + forecast only, not full 7-day history), requiring either a workaround (e.g., polling/caching) or fallback to a different API like IQAir or WAQI. Vue 3 + SVG is appropriate for the constraints and should comfortably fit under 1500 lines with a single-command local dev server (vite).

The plan is solid and well-scoped—no-build HTML/CSS/JS with Chart.js is appropriate for this assignment. Main risk: IQAir free tier has strict rate limits (10 calls/min, 30/day) and may require API key registration; verify this works with the assignment's "local, single command" requirement and consider fallback data or a local mock if the API becomes bottleneck. Otherwise, 4–5 days is realistic, leaving comfortable buffer before the 7-day deadline.

history: D3.js (powerful but steeper learning curve, larger footprint)

verdict.notes: D3.js is overkill for a simple 7-day trend chart and will consume ~200-300 lines just for setup/learning, leaving less room for polish. Consider Chart.js or even native Canvas instead—it's 80% as capable with 20% of the complexity, better fitting the 1500-line constraint and giving buffer for error handling, city search UX, and data fetching robustness.

branches.option: Plain HTML5 Canvas (most control, most code required)

history: Plain HTML5 Canvas (most control, most code required)

verdict.notes: The plan is feasible and meets all constraints, but Canvas for charting is a time-intensive choice that risks hitting the 1500-line limit or cutting corners on polish. Consider a lightweight charting library (Chart.js ~30KB) to reclaim dev time for better data handling, error states, and city search UX—vanilla Canvas can consume 200-300 lines just for axis rendering and tooltips.
